title: Kashiwara conjugation for twisted D-modules
speaker: Patrick Brosnan
field_msc: 32 Several complex variables and analytic spaces
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Commuting holomorphic and antiholomorphic operators on distributions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a complex manifold X, its conjugate Xbar, distributions on the underlying smooth manifold, and the key fact that D_X and D_Xbar commute inside the sheaf of smooth differential operators and both act on distributions.

proof_stages.label: Untwisted Kashiwara conjugation
proof_stages.summary: After recalling regular holonomic D-modules, perverse sheaves, and Riemann-Hilbert, the functor K(M)=Hom_{D_X}(M,DB_{X_R}) is defined as a D_Xbar-module and Kashiwara's equivalence RH(D_X) -> RH(D_Xbar) is stated, together with its distinction from ordinary conjugation of operators.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological classification of twisted differential operators
proof_stages.summary: Twisted differential operators are introduced as filtered sheaves locally isomorphic to D_X, their first-order part gives an extension 0 -> O_X -> F_1A -> T_X -> 0, and their isomorphism classes are identified with H^1(DO_X), with compact Kahler and flag-variety special cases noted.

proof_stages.label: Dolbeault representative and embedding of D_X^lambda
proof_stages.summary: Using a Dolbeault resolution of Omega_X^{>=1}, the speaker represents a twist by a closed (1,1)-form lambda, defines the first-order sheaf E_lambda by the equation W(f)=lambda(v,W), and states that it generates the corresponding TDO D_X^lambda inside Diff_{X_R}, locally as conjugation by a potential.

proof_stages.label: Twisted Kashiwara conjugation and polarizations
proof_stages.summary: The embedded algebras D_X^lambda and D_Xbar^{-lambda} are shown to commute on distributions, giving K(M)=Hom_{D_X^lambda}(M,DB_{X_R}) as a D_Xbar^{-lambda}-module and an equivalence on regular holonomic modules, after which bar conjugation and the pure-imaginary condition for polarizations are explained.

proof_stages.label: Hermitian forms and the Schmidt-Vilonen sign conjecture
proof_stages.summary: A polarization plus a volume form produces a Hermitian pairing on global sections, and in the flag-variety setting for real reductive groups the Schmidt-Vilonen conjecture predicts sign (-1)^p on the Hodge-filtered orthogonal pieces of K-equivariant Hodge modules.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional checks of the sign pattern
proof_stages.summary: In the SL_2/P^1 setting the conjectural signs are checked by explicit pairings: first for the delta module using derivatives of the standard volume density, and then for a nonintegral twisted module using beta/gamma-function integrals.

examples.name: Punctured affine line and the delta module
examples.note: The exact sequence O_X -> O_X(*0) -> D_X delta_0 on X=A^1 is used to make untwisted Kashiwara conjugation concrete, with the delta term seen from the distributional identity involving dbar(1/z); it illustrates rather than generates the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: he writes the A1 punctured-line exact sequence, identifies the quotient with the delta module, applies Kashiwara conjugation, and computes dbar(zbar/z) and dbar(1/z) to exhibit the delta function

passes.reason: The quotient is the point-supported delta D-module in the basic punctured-line exact sequence, used only to illustrate Kashiwara conjugation.
the example includes the point-supported delta D-module, a collapsed/minimal holonomic module used to illustrate Kashiwara conjugation rather than to refute a claim
the delta quotient is a point-supported skyscraper D-module in a standard one-dimensional local model used to illustrate conjugation.

The delta D-module in the punctured-line calculation is a point-supported minimal holonomic module, used only to illustrate Kashiwara conjugation.

examples.name: Twisted differential operators from a line bundle
examples.note: Differential operators on a line bundle are mentioned as the primordial TDO example and later as pure-imaginary, polarizable twists; this serves to orient the definitions but is not used to prove the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites differential operators on a line bundle as the primordial/known source of twists and states facts about their classes and polarizability without working through a specific line-bundle example

passes.reason: Twisted differential operators coming from a line bundle are the standard integral family of TDOs, cited as the primordial example.

examples.name: Delta-function Hodge module on P^1
examples.note: For X=P^1 and M=D_X delta_0, the speaker computes the Hermitian pairing on derivatives of the delta function and recovers the predicted (-1)^p signs, while emphasizing dependence on the standard volume form.
examples.engagement_reason: for M=Dx·delta on P1 he gives the Hodge filtration and explicitly computes the pairing of derivatives of the delta function, deriving the diagonal values and signs

passes.reason: The Hodge module is a delta/skyscraper module supported at a single point of P^1, used to compute the predicted signs.

examples.name: Nonintegral twisted P^1 module M_c
examples.note: A twisted D-module generated by a nonintegral parameter c on P^1 is paired by explicit polar-coordinate integrals whose gamma-function formula shows the alternating signature pattern already predicted by the conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: he defines the nonintegral twisted P1 module Mc, describes its filtered global sections, evaluates the resulting integrals via polar coordinates/Gamma functions, and analyzes the sign alternation
